metadata.career_relevance: TDD is the mark of a senior engineer. Kent Beck (creator of JUnit and XP) described TDD as 'the courage to refactor'. ThoughtWorks, Pivotal, and every agile team that ships reliably practices TDD. It prevents regression bugs, produces clean APIs (because you design from the caller's perspective), and gives confidence to refactor. In interviews, writing tests first signals seniority. After this topic, you have the complete professional Java testing toolkit.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll write tests before code, work in small Red-Green-Refactor cycles, use triangulation to drive implementation, let tests guide class design, and refactor safely inside the green zone.

concept_explanation.when_not_tdd:
**When TDD is less useful:**

TDD works best for:
- Business logic (discount calculations, validation, state machines)
- Service layer code
- Algorithms and data transformations

Less useful for:
- Exploratory code (spike solutions — learn, then throw away)
- UI layout and rendering
- Database migrations
- Configuration wiring
- Already-tested framework code (no need to test Spring's @Transactional)

**The pragmatic approach:** TDD for all business logic; test-after for integration/wiring where TDD is impractical. A reasonable target: 80% of business logic is TDD; 20% is tested after.

visual_aid.description: Circular workflow diagram: three states in a loop — RED (red circle, test written, code missing), GREEN (green circle, minimum code written, test passes), REFACTOR (blue circle, code improved, tests still pass). Arrow from RED to GREEN labeled 'write minimum code'. Arrow from GREEN to REFACTOR labeled 'improve code'. Arrow from REFACTOR back to RED labeled 'write next test'. In the center: 'repeat every 2-5 min'. Outside the circle: examples at each stage — RED: '@Test void discount_qty10...' (test code with no implementation), GREEN: 'class Calc { double disc(int n) { return 0.10; } }' (minimal), REFACTOR: 'if (n>=50) 0.20; if (n>=10) 0.10; else 0' (complete logic). Bottom: triangulation diagram — single test → fake implementation possible; two tests → real logic required.
visual_aid.alt_text: Red-Green-Refactor cycle with examples at each stage, and triangulation forcing real implementation

worked_example.scenario: Build a `ShoppingCart` from scratch using TDD. Each cycle adds one behaviour. Show exactly 6 TDD cycles: empty cart, add item, add two items, remove item, apply discount, get item count.
worked_example.problem_statement: Build ShoppingCart.java using TDD — write tests first, implement minimum code to pass each test, refactor between cycles. Final cart supports: add, remove, getTotal, getItemCount, applyDiscount.

step_by_step_solution.action: Cycle 1 — empty cart has zero total (RED → GREEN)
Cycle 2 — add one item, total changes (RED → GREEN)
Cycle 3 — two items accumulate (RED → GREEN, already works)
Cycle 4 — remove item reduces total (RED → GREEN → REFACTOR)
Cycle 6 — item count (trivial, but spec'd) (RED → GREEN)

worked_example.expected_output:
Test Results:
✓ emptyCart_totalIsZero
✓ addItem_singleItem_total
✓ addTwoItems_totalIsSum
✓ removeItem_existingItem_reduced
✓ applyDiscount_10pct_reduced
✓ getItemCount_threeItems

6 tests passed, 0 failed
things_to_notice: Each test was written BEFORE the code that makes it pass. The test defines what the code must do — not the other way around.
Cycle 1's implementation (`return 0.0`) was intentionally hardcoded. This is correct TDD — write the MINIMUM to pass. Don't write `double total = 0; ... return total;` until a test forces you to.

Cycle 3 (two items) was already GREEN when the test was written — the implementation from Cycle 2 already handled it. This is normal in TDD and perfectly fine. Write the test anyway; it documents the behavior.

The refactoring in Cycle 4 changed the internal representation from a simple `double total` to `Map<String, Double> items`. The tests stayed GREEN throughout this structural change — that's the safety net at work.

applyDiscount uses `items.replaceAll()` — a clean functional API. This emerged naturally because the Map representation, chosen for removeItem, also makes discount elegant. Good design flows from the tests.

The final Cart class has 5 public methods, all designed from the tests' perspective. The API is clean because it was designed by someone who needed to USE it (the test), not someone thinking about implementation.

industry_examples.use_case: ThoughtWorks (founded by Martin Fowler, practitioners of XP) mandates TDD for all production code. Each feature starts as a failing acceptance test (high-level) and failing unit tests (low-level). Code is written only to pass these tests. Refactoring is continuous. CI runs all tests on every commit. A codebase built with TDD has near-zero 'test debt' — tests exist for everything important.

The Spring Boot team develops all auto-configurations test-first. They write integration tests for the desired behavior (e.g., 'if H2 is on the classpath and no DataSource bean is defined, auto-configure one'), then write the auto-configuration code to make the test pass. This ensures every configuration is validated by tests that reflect real user scenarios.

Many Flipkart and Zerodha engineering interviews now ask candidates to write tests first and code second. The ability to think from the caller's perspective, handle edge cases before coding them, and work in small steps separates junior from senior engineers. A candidate who writes tests before code demonstrates design thinking.

applications.interview_relevance:
TDD in interviews:

- **'How do you ensure code quality?'** → Test-driven development — write tests first, implement minimum code to pass, refactor with test safety.
- **'How do you approach a new feature?'** → Start with failing tests that define the expected behaviour. Write code only to pass those tests. Refactor once green.
- **'What is the Red-Green-Refactor cycle?'** → Red: write a failing test. Green: write minimum code to pass. Refactor: improve code while keeping tests green.
- Some companies (ThoughtWorks, Pivotal) do pair-programming TDD sessions in their interviews.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily TDD workflow:

- New feature: write failing test first
- New class: let first test define constructor and first method
- Bug fix: write a test that demonstrates the bug, then fix
- Refactoring: only refactor when you have full test coverage
- Red stays red longer than 5 minutes? Your step was too big — back up
- Tests pass immediately (never red)? Your test didn't add anything useful

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Writing all the tests for a class before writing any code — 10+ failing tests, then implementing everything at once. This is not TDD.
common_gaps.remediation: TDD is one test at a time. Write one test, make it pass, refactor, repeat. The RED phase should be ONE failing test, not ten. The discipline is the short cycle — minutes, not hours.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Multiple tests written before any production code exists

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Writing the 'full' implementation when a simpler one would pass the current test — skipping the minimum-code step.
common_gaps.remediation: In Green, write ONLY enough code to pass the current failing test. If you can return a hardcoded value and pass the test, do that. The next test will force you further. This discipline catches design assumptions early.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Implementation always complete after first test; subsequent tests never fail

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Going directly from Green to the next Red without refactoring — accumulating 'test debt' in the implementation.
common_gaps.remediation: After every Green, ask: is this code clean? Rename variables, extract methods, remove duplication. The tests protect you. A 2-minute refactor after each cycle prevents hours of later cleanup.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Duplicated code, poor naming, long methods building up over TDD cycles

key_takeaways: TDD cycle: Red (write failing test) → Green (minimum code to pass) → Refactor (improve) → Repeat
Write ONE test at a time — not all tests upfront
Green phase: write MINIMUM code — hardcoded returns are OK if they pass the test
Triangulation: add a second test to force real logic when the first can be faked
Refactor in Green — tests protect against breaking changes
TDD reveals design problems — if a test is hard to write, the design is wrong
Baby steps: each cycle 2-5 minutes; each test adds one behaviour
TDD produces better APIs — designed from the caller's perspective
Exception paths: write the test for the exception FIRST
TDD is not suitable for everything — exploratory, UI, migrations are less suited
The courage to refactor comes from test coverage

self_check_questions.question: You're TDDing a `StringReverser.reverse(String s)` class. First test: `assertEquals('olleh', reverser.reverse('hello'))`. You implement `return 'olleh'` (hardcoded). Why is this acceptable? What do you do next?
self_check_questions.answer: It's acceptable because the current test passes — and TDD's Green rule says write only enough to pass. The hardcoded value is the minimum code. Next: add a SECOND test that triangulates: `assertEquals('ydaer', reverser.reverse('ready'))`. Now 'olleh' fails for 'ready'. This forces a real implementation: `new StringBuilder(s).reverse().toString()`. Two tests triangulate the general logic that one test could fake.

self_check_questions.question: You're in the Green phase (all tests pass) and realize the Cart.addItem() stores items in a List but you now need removeItem(). Should you refactor to a Map before writing the removeItem test?
self_check_questions.answer: Yes! Refactoring in the Green zone is exactly right. You see the design improvement (Map makes removeItem clean), you have test coverage (existing tests will protect you), so refactor NOW: change List to Map, run tests (they should all still pass), then write the failing removeItem test. Never add new behavior and refactor at the same time — refactor in Green, add behavior by writing a Red test.

self_check_questions.question: A colleague says 'TDD is slow — writing tests before code doubles the time'. How do you respond?
self_check_questions.answer: TDD feels slower per feature but is faster overall. Time saved: (1) debugging — bugs caught immediately by the failing test, not hours later in production; (2) regression protection — every refactor is safe with full test coverage; (3) design — TDD prevents untestable code from being written, reducing future rework; (4) documentation — tests document exactly how the code should be used. Studies (including Microsoft and IBM internal research) show TDD increases initial development time by 15-35% but reduces bug rates by 40-90%. Net: faster total delivery because less time debugging and fixing production issues.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Module 12: Unit Testing with JUnit 5 is complete! You've built the full testing toolkit: JUnit 5 basics, parameterized tests, Mockito mocking, I/O and HTTP testing, and TDD. From Module 13, the course moves into specialized Java topics: Spring Boot, database access with JPA, and building production REST APIs. Every module from here forward builds on the testing skills you've just mastered.
